title: "In Vino Veritas" — In wine there is truth.
story:
Sometimes the hardest story to tell is your own. It's either too short and doesn't do it enough justice, or you tell every moment you ever lived and find yourself rivaling Victor Hugo. We'll try to strike a balance. We'll try to keep it real.

So how did a Pakistani immigrant from Jersey and a small town girl from Ohio end up building a wine cellar in Dallas, TX and ultimately launching the Kazmi Cellars Experience? Well, neither of us grew up with wine at all.

## How It Started

I was raised strictly religious, no alcohol to be found. I needed a job to pay for college in Jersey and walked up and down George Street in New Brunswick applying anywhere from a deli to a cellular store. You have to have a few grey hairs to remember that one. I ended up applying at a restaurant called SoHo on George. The nicest restaurant I had ever been to up until that point was Pizza Hut when they had sit down restaurants, and I had certainly never walked into a place like this. Immaculate table settings, beautiful wood tables, the art, the colors, waiters in ties. I walked in wearing a t-shirt and jeans with a sweat ridden body and a stack of resumes.

They were looking for a host. I didn't even know what that was, but if it paid I would apply. I sat at the bar and one of my future groomsmen was behind it polishing glasses. He offered me a glass of water while I filled out the application. Long story short, they hired me.

Slowly into my role I started to see the romance of fine dining. People getting dressed up, celebrating their most important moments, meals that had courses, lunches that lasted for hours. And then came the wine. The manager was a superb wine expert, not a sommelier but could have been. At family meal, the meals for staff before each shift, she would let the servers taste new wines she was introducing. That's literally how I had my first sip of wine.

Like every college kid I was a Miller Lite and Jack Daniels kind of guy. It was a lot easier on the wallet. I didn't particularly care for the taste at first but I was fascinated by how in depth she would go into each wine, sometimes before we even tasted it. It's alcohol in a glass, why was she describing it as if it were a living being? But it put the hook in me. If you don't know, I'm a romantic at heart, and frankly outside of love I don't know a more romantic thing than wine.

I started to take more interest in wine than some of the servers. I also very quickly realized I was making $10 an hour and the waiters, well, let's just say they were making bank. With literally never having served even a glass of water, on sheer observation alone, I begged my way into a server role about a year later. That was the first and most important chapter.

## Rachel's Story

Rachel's path was different. Growing up in Ohio and going to school in Dayton, her focus was making a life for herself, a path that would lead away from small town beginnings, not out of shame but out of ambition. She is the brains of the operation, if you know what I mean.

She took the biggest leap of her life when she landed her first job in Northern California. A small town girl who had never even been on a plane before was suddenly moving to the big city. Her open mindedness and her career brought her to countries all over Southeast Asia and cities all over the US. Eventually making her way to Southern California, she settled in beautiful Santa Monica and started enjoying some of the finer things in life, wine being one of them. Was she in love with it? Probably not. Frankly, at that point, neither was I. More infatuated than anything else.

True love came when we joined forces.

## When Two Roads Became One

Rachel eventually moved to Jersey for work. I had just moved back after a few years in San Diego and we met in the town where my wine journey started, New Brunswick, NJ. One day maybe I'll tell the story of how we met and came to be, it's a great one, but for now I'll try to keep it short. Yes, this is my version of short.

As we got to know each other, we started enjoying wine together. I was out of the restaurant business and into the insurance industry, which I'm still in, but we would visit my old restaurant friends and enjoy great wine together. It was a common interest, but interest was all that it was.

Then we got married and moved to LA. The interest turned to curiosity, the curiosity turned into a passion, and the passion turned into a love affair. We found ourselves in Santa Barbara. I suppose all roads for us lead to Santa Barbara, not the town, the wine country. You can read all about that chapter when we talk about Santa Barbara as a region. That trip changed everything.

## Dallas, The Cellar, and How We Got Here

So how did we end up in Texas? Like anyone else from LA, I suppose, cost of living. We were expecting our first child and barely getting by. We decided to move somewhere more affordable and closer to Rachel's sister who had already settled in Dallas. We started a new life, literally kicking and screaming, one because we didn't want to leave LA, and two because our daughter was born a few months after we arrived.

Funny side note, when we moved we had the movers take everything except our wine. We had about 300 bottles and we packed both of our cars. My sister and brother in law flew out to LA and we drove convoy style to Dallas with nothing but wine. Looking back, that was probably a sign we'd have a cellar one day.

Dallas has been great to us. Not the sexiest place on earth but a great place to raise a family and both of our careers have flourished. This allowed us to have a real home and in Dallas, because you actually have space, you get two dining rooms. Yes, two. One in the kitchen and one formal. As parents of two beautiful daughters, the formal dining room wasn't exactly getting much use.

One evening I cleared the Amazon boxes off the table, decided to cook Rachel a nice meal, and opened some killer wine. As the wine flowed and obviously went to my head, I found myself looking around the room and had an epiphany. We should build a wine cellar, I declared, as if I was preaching for independence. Rachel laughed it off. Clearly the wine was speaking for me, but it worked.

A few weeks later Rachel said, let's do it. Like any husband, I panicked. What did I agree to and when did I agree to it? For all I knew I had agreed to a full body wax. "Let's build a cellar," she declared. Here, here.

Before I knew it she, being a former architecture student, the brains of the operation remember, was mapping out the entire room. We had a handyman do the work, she did the design, and within a few months we had a 900 bottle wine cellar with its own cooling unit, racks, a dining table and all.

We started traveling more, Tuscany, the Rhône, Burgundy, shipping cases of wine back home. We leaned heavily on our wine memberships, Dragonette, Jada, Paradigm, Foley Family, Chimney Rock, and on and on. We overfilled the cellar to roughly 1,100 bottles and threw elaborate dinner parties where the wine was never just for us. It was always to share. A glass of wine can last forever if you share it as a memory, and we have a lot of great memories.

## The Table That Built a Community

As my career in Risk Management grew, so did something I never quite expected from a corporate life, genuine human connection. Entertaining clients and brokers became a big part of my role and somewhere along the way the dinner table became one of my favorite places on earth. Whether it was a long dinner in a private room, a casual glass at a hotel bar after a long conference day, or a bottle shared at someone's home, some of my closest friendships in this world were born over wine. Clients became friends, brokers became brothers and sisters, and the common thread running through almost every meaningful moment was a glass in hand and a story being told.

The actual work of being a Risk Management professional has brought me real satisfaction, I won't pretend otherwise. But if I'm being honest, the relationships I've built over breaking bread and sharing wine are the ones that have filled me up the most. Every bottle opened in that context was never just about the wine. It was about the person sitting across from me. That's a lesson wine taught me long before I ever had a cellar to put it in.

## Why Kazmi Cellars

Then 2026 hit. We lost my brother, a man who loved life and everyone in it. And I found out I needed a hip replacement at 47. These things bring pain, emotionally and physically, but if channeled right they can also bring perspective.

I had grand plans for my downtime recovering from surgery. The possibilities were endless, and believe it or not, pouring my soul into Kazmi Cellars was not at the top of the list. It is now.

The perspective I've gained is this, as much as I love my career, and I know, I actually do, I found myself tumbling into wine. It's like walking down a slippery hill. At first you just lose your balance, but before you know it you're rolling. Only the hill I'm rolling down is full of beautiful memories of Rachel and I, of friends and family, and even strangers we've been blessed enough to share our passion with. Every tumble I take down that hill is like living an old memory, created and surrounded by wine.

We are not sommeliers and we will never claim to be. But somewhere between that first sip at SoHo on George Street and the 1,100 bottles currently threatening to take over our cellar, we picked up a thing or two. Enough to share, enough to guide, and always enough to keep learning.

The passion that Rachel and I have is not necessarily for wine itself, but for the world that wine surrounds itself with. The earth, the beauty, the complexities, the highs, the lows, the friendships, and ultimately for us, the romance. How could we not share this with others?

So here we are. Kazmi Cellars at your doorstep. We want to create a space where wine is not the end goal. It's the experiences that wine creates, and the sharing of those experiences with anyone who will pull up a chair.

Come sit. Learn something. Teach us something. But most importantly, just come and sit.

Journey Wine With Us. It would be our honor, and as the Europeans say it, our great pleasure. 🍷
